common_name: Giant Leopard Plant (Bold Round-Leaf Deep-Shade Fall-Blooming Gulf South Perennial)

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Farfugium performs best with consistent moisture — the large leaves can wilt dramatically in dry conditions before recovering overnight, but persistent drought causes leaf scorch and decline.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Giant Leopard Plant (Farfugium japonicum) is one of the most dramatic and valuable shade perennials for Gulf South gardens — a bold-textured, evergreen perennial producing very large (8–12 inch across), round to kidney-shaped, dark glossy green leaves on long stalks, creating a striking, tropical-looking effect in deep shade positions where most ornamentals struggle to survive let alone thrive. The leaves are among the largest of any commonly grown Gulf South shade perennial, providing a dramatic bold-texture contrast in planting combinations with fine-textured shade companions like ferns, mondo grass, and liriope. In fall (October–November in zone 9), Farfugium produces clusters of bright yellow, daisy-like flowers on tall stalks above the foliage — a welcome burst of color in the shade garden during a season when few other shade plants are flowering. This timing — fall bloom in deep shade — makes Farfugium essentially unique among commonly grown shade perennials and provides an important ornamental bonus beyond the year-round foliage display. Farfugium is particularly associated with Gulf South garden tradition and appears prominently in New Orleans and coastal Mississippi shade gardens. The plant is adaptable to a range of shade conditions, from bright indirect light to fairly deep shade under large trees, maintaining good leaf size and color across this range. Division of established clumps every 3–5 years maintains vigor and provides plants for expanding shade garden areas.

common_name: Spotted Leopard Plant (Yellow-Spotted Bold-Leaf Shade Perennial for Gulf South Gardens)

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; the yellow spotting of Aureomaculatum is caused by viral variegation and is therefore heritable but not transmissible to other plants — propagate only by division to maintain the spotted pattern.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Aureomaculatum is the spotted form of Farfugium japonicum — the same bold-textured, large-leaved shade perennial as the green species but with distinctive irregular yellow spots and blotches distributed randomly across the dark green leaf surface, resembling paint splattered across the leaves and creating a strikingly unusual variegation pattern not found in other commonly grown shade perennials. The yellow spots of Aureomaculatum are the result of a viral variegation — genetically stable but caused by a specific viral interaction in the plant tissue — which means the spotting pattern is propagated faithfully by division but cannot be transmitted to adjacent plants. Each leaf produces a uniquely arranged set of yellow spots, and the number and size of spots varies from leaf to leaf, creating an irregular, artistically random variegation that is more visually interesting than uniformly-patterned variegation. The spotted pattern brightens deep shade positions significantly — the yellow spots appear luminous in low-light conditions, functioning like scattered points of light in the dark garden. Otherwise, the cultural requirements and fall yellow flowers of Aureomaculatum are identical to the green-leaved species. In Gulf South shade gardens, the spotted form provides a higher ornamental value than the straight species and is worth seeking out from specialty nurseries and plant societies. Both forms are equally cold-hardy and reliable in zone 7–10.

common_name: Ozark Beauty Strawberry (Reliable Everbearing Strawberry for Home Garden Production)

planting.note: Plant bare-root crowns in late winter to early spring (January–February in zone 9) in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun; in zone 9, fall planting (October–November) is often preferred as it allows plants to establish during the cool season and produce a heavy spring crop before summer heat declines production.

directions: Plant bare-root crowns in late winter–early spring (or fall in zone 9) in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun; Ozark Beauty is one of the most widely grown and long-proven everbearing strawberry varieties for home gardens — producing two to three crops annually (spring, summer, and fall in cooler regions; spring and fall in the Gulf South) versus the single annual spring crop of June-bearing types. The everbearing fruiting habit provides strawberries across a much longer season than June-bearers, making Ozark Beauty the preferred choice for home gardeners who want a sustained harvest rather than the concentrated single-season production of June-bearing varieties. The medium-to-large fruits are bright red, firm, and sweet — not quite the exceptional fresh flavor of the best June-bearing varieties (like Chandler) but reliably good across all crop flushes. The everbearing habit is maintained by growing the plant without harvesting runners during the first year, allowing energy to concentrate in fruit production rather than vegetative spread. In zone 9, Ozark Beauty performs best when planted in fall (October–November) to allow establishment during the cool season — spring transplants in zone 9 have limited time to establish before summer heat reduces productivity. Mulching with straw or pine straw conserves soil moisture, maintains even soil temperature, and keeps fruit clean. Remove runners from plants dedicated to fruit production to maintain fruiting energy.

common_name: White Beautyberry (White-Berried Form of Native American Beautyberry Shrub)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; White Beautyberry produces the most prolific berry clusters in partial shade with morning sun — deep shade reduces berry production significantly, while full sun requires consistent moisture to prevent drought stress.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; White Beautyberry is the white-fruited selection of Callicarpa americana — the native American beautyberry that is one of the most striking native fruiting shrubs of the Gulf South, now in the unusual and elegant white-berry form that provides a distinct ornamental alternative to the standard vivid-purple berry clusters. The white (ivory to pale cream-white) berry clusters of 'Alba' are arranged in the same dense, tightly-packed rings around the stems at each leaf axil as the purple species — the distinctive whorled ring pattern that makes beautyberry unmistakable — but the white color creates a different visual effect: quieter and more sophisticated in combination with dark green foliage, and particularly effective in evening garden settings or combined with dark purple or burgundy companions where the contrast is strong. The berry clusters are produced abundantly in August through November, persisting on the bare stems into winter after the leaves fall, providing food for at least 40 species of songbirds and game birds. The leaves of American beautyberry contain callicarpenal, a compound with documented mosquito-repellent activity that has long been used in folk tradition. Beautyberry grows as a loose, arching shrub that benefits from hard pruning in late winter — cutting back by one-third to one-half stimulates vigorous new growth that produces the most prolific berry clusters in the same year. Self-sows in favorable conditions.

common_name: Mammoth Long Island Dill (Tall Classic Dill for Pickling and Abundant Seed Heads)

planting.note: Direct sow seeds thinly in fall (September–November in zone 9) or very early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mammoth Long Island can be direct-sown in fall in zone 9 for winter and spring herb harvest and seed production — fall-sown plants often overwinter as rosettes and produce the largest seed heads of any sowing time.

directions: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mammoth Long Island is the tall, large-seed-head dill cultivar traditionally grown for pickling and dill seed production — producing the largest seed heads and highest seed yields of common dill varieties, along with an abundance of feathery foliage for fresh and dried dill weed use throughout the season. The name reflects the cultivar's origins in Long Island, New York pickling operations where large seed heads were needed to efficiently pack dill into pickle jars — those large, flat compound umbels of yellow flowers and, later, of aromatic dill seed are the defining feature of the variety, producing proportionally more seed per plant than dwarf and compact varieties. The tall habit (3–5 feet) provides visual presence in the herb garden and creates the feathery, vertical texture that makes dill an attractive ornamental companion for low-growing vegetable crops. In Gulf South zone 9, dill is best grown as a cool-season herb: fall-sown plants establish through the cool months, producing foliage for harvest through winter and spring, then flower and set seed in March–April before summer heat. Fall-grown plants self-sow freely, naturalizing the dill into the garden as a self-renewing herb. Dill foliage is the host plant for black swallowtail butterfly larvae — accepting this graciously means dill does double duty as both herb and butterfly garden plant.
